perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, the rights and obligations of the parties hereto, and any claims or disputes thereto, shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without reference to conflict of law principles. Venue for any proceedings not subject to arbitration under this Agreement shall be in the state and federal courts located in New York, New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, the rights and obligations of the parties hereto, and any claims or disputes thereto, shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without reference to conflict of law principles. Venue for any proceedings not subject to arbitration under this Agreement shall be in the state courts located in Atlanta, Georgia.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies New York law will govern and New York courts will have venue. This modification changes the governing law to Delaware and changes the venue to Atlanta, Georgia. This omission potentially violates consumer protection laws depending on where the consumer resides and where the services are provided. Delaware law may be less favorable to consumers than New York law, and requiring consumers to litigate in Atlanta, Georgia, regardless of their location, could be considered an unfair or deceptive practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 349 prohibits deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any business, trade or commerce or in the furnishing of any service in this state and states that the injured party can recover actual damages or fifty dollars, whichever is greater. By changing the governing law to Delaware, and changing the venue to Atlanta, Georgia, the contract may be attempting to circumvent this New York law to the detriment of New York consumers, which can be deemed deceptive.
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the governing law from New York to Delaware and the venue from New York to Atlanta, Georgia. The Law Explanation states that this change "may be attempting to circumvent this New York law to the detriment of New York consumers, which can be deemed deceptive." This aligns with NY GBL § 349, which prohibits deceptive acts or practices. The omission of New York law and venue directly contradicts the consumer protection intended by NY GBL § 349, potentially harming New York consumers by forcing them to litigate under less favorable laws and in a distant location. The snippet "ARTICLE 22-A Consumer Protection From Deceptive Acts and Practices up ARTICLE 22-A Consumer Protection From Deceptive Acts and Practices next SECTION 349-A" indicates this section deals with consumer protection from deceptive practices, directly relevant to the potential circumvention described.

perturbation.original_text: HSWI shall maintain complete and accurate records to support and document the Royalties owed under this Agreement in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles consistently applied and to document compliance with the terms of this Agreement. HSWI shall also provide reasonable assistance to World Book or its designated agent to conduct audits to confirm the payments hereunder. Any such audit will be conducted upon [*] ([*]) days notice and during regular business hours, and shall be at [*] expense, unless such audit reveals a discrepancy of more than [*] percent ([*]%) in the total applicable amount reported by HSWI, in which case [*] shall pay for, or reimburse [*] the cost of, such audit. Any such audit shall be conducted by an independent certified public accounting firm which is not engaged in performing other work for World Book or its affiliates; which agrees to enter into a confidentiality agreement with HSWI; and which is not compensated in any manner of contingency arrangements on the basis of its findings. HSWI further agrees that until the expiration of [*] ([*]) year after the termination of this Agreement, HSWI will make available upon written request to World Book or any of its duly authorized representatives, this Agreement and books, documents, and records of HSWI that are necessary to verify the nature and extent of the revenue derived by HSWI from advertising related to the Content hereunder. No more than [*] audit may be conducted in any [*] month period, unless the then-most-recent audit reveals a discrepancy of more than [*] percent ([*]%) in the total applicable amount reported by HSWI.
perturbation.changed_text: HSWI shall maintain records to document compliance with the terms of this Agreement. HSWI shall provide assistance to World Book or its designated agent to conduct audits to confirm the payments hereunder. Any such audit will be conducted with reasonable notice and during business hours. HSWI further agrees that until the expiration of 1 year after the termination of this Agreement, HSWI will make available upon written request to World Book or any of its duly authorized representatives, this Agreement and records of HSWI that are necessary to verify the nature and extent of the revenue derived by HSWI from advertising related to the Content hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the audit clause. The original requires 'complete and accurate records' maintained according to GAAP. Removing this standard introduces ambiguity and makes it harder to verify royalties. Removing the specific terms of notice for audits and who pays for audits removes legal protection for World Book. The reduction of time available to audit from an unspecified amount to one year weakens World Book's ability to verify royalty payments.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 6001 - Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly applicable (as this is about *internal* business records to calculate royalties), this weakened audit provision makes it far easier to avoid paying applicable taxes due to inaccurate records, by not demanding full and accurate reporting compliant with GAAP.

Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the audit clause by removing the requirement for 'complete and accurate records' maintained according to GAAP. The original text also specifies notice and expense terms for audits, which are removed, weakening World Book's protections. The removal of these details creates ambiguity and makes it harder to verify royalty payments. The LLM explanation states that these changes make it easier to avoid paying applicable taxes due to inaccurate records. Snippet 1, 26 USC 6001, states that 'Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title...shall keep such records'. While the contract is not directly related to tax law, the changes make it harder for World Book to confirm the royalty payments, creating a contradiction in the spirit of the law that companies must keep records to verify what they owe.

perturbation.original_text: World Book hereby agrees to defend, indemnify and hold harmless HSWI, its Affiliates, and their officers, directors, agents, employees, advertisers, service providers, end users and related entities from all costs, damages, liabilities and expenses (including reasonable attorneys fees) arising out of: (a) any third party claim or action alleging that the Content, Affinities and Images (excluding any World Book Derivatives independently created by or on behalf of HSWI) in the manner delivered by World Book or as contemplated for HSWI's use hereunder infringes or misappropriates any Intellectual Property Rights of any third party; and/or (b) any third party claim or action arising out of World Book's material breach of this Agreement; provided that: (i) HSWI notifies World Book promptly in writing of any such threat, claim, and/or proceedings related thereto; (ii) World Book is given the opportunity to assume sole control over the defense and all related settlement negotiations, except that HSWI shall have sole authority over approval of any settlement that admits fault or liability on the part of HSWI; and, (iii) HSWI provides World Book with all reasonably necessary assistance, information and authority to perform the foregoing at World Book's expense.
perturbation.changed_text: World Book agrees to indemnify HSWI from costs and damages arising out of any claim alleging that the Content infringes any Intellectual Property Rights of any third party, provided that HSWI notifies World Book of any such claim.
perturbation.explanation: This modification severely limits the scope of World Book's indemnification obligations. Key omissions include: (1) Removing the explicit listing of HSWI's 'Affiliates, and their officers, directors, agents, employees, advertisers, service providers, end users and related entities' significantly narrows the scope of protection. (2) Removing the phrase 'liabilities and expenses (including reasonable attorneys fees)' limits the financial protection provided. (3) Removing the detailed conditions (i, ii, iii) regarding control of the defense and settlement negotiations hands all control and settlement authority to HSWI, even if it is harmful to World Book. This creates potential legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the contract is governed by New York law (see other perturbations), an indemnification clause such as this is likely to be litigated in California, where California Civil Code § 2778 implies certain duties of the indemnitee (HSWI) to cooperate with the indemnitor (World Book) and act in good faith. By omitting requirements for HSWI to allow World Book to control the defense and settlement, the modified contract creates a legal contradiction with the implied duties under California law, potentially limiting World Book's rights and increasing its exposure.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reque
perturbation.justification: The modified contract significantly reduces World Book's protection compared to the original, potentially contradicting implied duties under California law, specifically regarding control over defense and settlement. The original contract provided World Book the opportunity to control the defense. The modified text removes this, potentially limiting World Book's rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing CIV 2778, states that "the person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so;" This suggests a cooperative relationship where the indemnifying party (World Book) has some rights. The omission of this right in the modified text directly contradicts the spirit, creating a legal risk for World Book.
